explanation: The property states that globally, if the leak compensation feature is not active, it is always eventually possible for it to become active. This directly translates the 'user shall be able to enable the leak compensation feature at any time' aspect of the request. The 'at any time' is captured by the global operator (G), and 'able to enable' is captured by the eventual operator (F) applied to the state of the feature becoming active. This template covers one direction of the 'disable/enable' requirement (the 'enable' part from a disabled state).

The natural language requirement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies that the system should always (G) eventually (F) allow the user to enable this feature, and similarly, always eventually allow them to disable it. This is a responsiveness property often translated as `G (F enableLeakCompensation) & G (F !enableLeakCompensation)`. However, the provided LTL templates do not allow for a direct conjunction of two such 'always eventually' properties within a single translation. Therefore, this translation focuses on the 'enable' aspect, asserting that the system is always eventually in a state where the 'enable leak compensation' action can be performed. Template 19, `(G (bool_exp2 -> (F bool_exp3)))`, is chosen. By setting `bool_exp2` to a tautology (an expression that is always true, like `P | !P`), the template simplifies to `G (F bool_exp3)`. `bool_exp3` is then assigned the atomic proposition `enableLeakCompensation` to represent the ability to enable the feature.

This LTL property, derived from template 19, specifies that it is globally true (G) that if the 'enableLeakCompensation' feature is currently active (true), then it will eventually (F) be possible to disable it (become false, i.e., '!enableLeakCompensation'). This captures the aspect of "the user shall be able to disable the leak compensation feature at any time" by ensuring the system never gets permanently stuck in the enabled state, thus providing the ability to turn it off. A complementary property, 'G (!enableLeakCompensation -> F enableLeakCompensation)', would be needed to fully capture the ability to 'enable' it at any time, but only one translation can be provided per request.

The natural language statement implies that the 'leak compensation feature' (represented by 'leakCompensationActive') should not be stuck in either an active or inactive state. The phrase 'able to disable/enable at any time' indicates that if the feature is currently inactive, it should always be possible to enable it, and vice versa. This can be expressed as a liveness property: 'Globally, if the feature is in one state, it will eventually be possible to transition to the other state.' This translation specifically captures the ability to enable the feature if it is currently inactive. It states that at any point in time, if leak compensation is not active, it will eventually become active. This uses template 19, where 'bool_exp2' is the condition 'leak compensation is not active' and 'bool_exp3' is the desired state 'leak compensation is active'.

The property states that globally, if the leak compensation feature is currently disabled, then it will always eventually be possible to enable it. This captures the aspect of the user being able to 'enable' the feature when it's not active. The 'at any time' part is captured by the 'G' (Globally) operator, meaning this possibility is always present. The full intent of 'disable/enable' would ideally require a symmetric property (i.e., if enabled, it can eventually be disabled), but only one template can be chosen.

The property states that it is always globally true that eventually, the leak compensation feature will be enabled (G F enableLeakCompensation). This captures the 'enable' part of 'able to disable/enable' and 'at any time', implying that the system offers the continuous possibility to activate this feature. While it does not explicitly capture the 'disable' part (i.e., G F !enableLeakCompensation), it ensures the feature is not permanently disabled, aligning with the spirit of controllability.

The LTL property `G (!enableLeakCompensation -> F enableLeakCompensation)` states that if the leak compensation feature is currently disabled (`!enableLeakCompensation`), it is always the case that it can eventually be enabled (`F enableLeakCompensation`). This captures the part of the requirement that the user shall be able to enable the feature at any time it is disabled. The natural language phrase "disable/enable" implies a symmetrical property (that if it's enabled, it can also eventually be disabled), but given the constraint to provide a single LTL formula, this expresses the continuous availability of the feature for activation.

The natural language "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies that the system should always allow the state of the leak compensation feature to be changed. Specifically, the phrase "at any time" suggests a global possibility, captured by the 'Always Eventually' (GF) operator. The phrase "disable/enable" refers to the ability to control the state of the feature. Given the atomic proposition 'leakCompensationActive' indicates the state of the feature, we interpret the 'enable' part of the request as always eventually being able to reach a state where leak compensation is active. Template 19, 'G (bool_exp2 -> (F bool_exp3))', is chosen. By setting 'bool_exp2' to 'true', the formula simplifies to 'G (F bool_exp3)', meaning that 'bool_exp3' is always eventually true. 'bool_exp3' is set to 'leakCompensationActive' to represent the feature being enabled. This LTL property thus states that the leak compensation feature can always eventually be enabled. The 'disable' aspect, which would translate to 'G (F !leakCompensationActive)', is not directly covered by a single, separate template choice as per the instructions, which restrict to one template.

The LTL property states that 'Globally, if a given condition (bool_exp1) is true, then eventually bool_exp3 will be true.' In this context, 'at any time' is captured by setting bool_exp1 to 'True', indicating that the capability is always available. The 'enable' part of 'disable/enable' is captured by bool_exp3, which represents 'leakCompensationActive', meaning the leak compensation feature can always eventually become active. The 'disable' part is not explicitly captured by a single template in the provided list, as a full 'disable/enable at any time' property would ideally require a conjunction of two LTL formulas (G(F P) and G(F !P)). However, given the constraint to choose only one template, capturing the ability to enable the feature at any time is a strong and relevant interpretation of the prompt.

The natural language phrase "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies that the system continuously allows the user to change the state of the leak compensation feature. Since "enableLeakCompensation" is an atomic proposition indicating the user's action to enable the feature, and assuming that "disabling" is either a direct counterpart action (not explicitly provided as an AP) or implied by the ability to toggle the feature, the most direct interpretation is that the *action* of enabling the feature is always eventually possible. The LTL formula G(F(bool_exp3)), obtained from template 19 by setting bool_exp2 to a tautology, ensures that from any state, it is always eventually possible for 'enableLeakCompensation' to occur. This captures the 'able to enable... at any time' part of the requirement. While 'disable' isn't explicitly covered by a separate AP, the common understanding of 'enable/disable' features suggests a toggle, where the ability to enable implies the ability to interact with and change the feature's state.
